items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.title: Incidence and Risk Factors for Dog Bite Infections
items.content: Dog bites are a common cause of animal-bite wounds in the United States, with over 4.7 million incidents annually. Approximately 15-20% of these bites become infected. Children, particularly boys aged 5–9 years old, have the highest incidence rate at 6 bites per 1000 population.
keyPoints: Dog bites account for >80% of all animal-bite wounds in the U.S.
Annual incidence: 300 dog and cat bites per 100,000 population
15–20% of dog bites become infected
Highest incidence among boys aged 5-9 years old at 6 bites per 1000 population
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Microbiology of Dog-Bite Infections
items.content: Dog-bite infections typically present with pain, cellulitis, and purulent discharge 8–24 hours post-injury. Common pathogens include Pasteurella species, β-hemolytic streptococci, Staphylococcus (including MRSA), Neisseria weaveri, Eikenella corrodens, and Capnocytophaga canimorsus.
keyPoints: Infection manifests 8–24 hours post-bite with pain, cellulitis, purulent discharge
Common pathogens: Pasteurella species, β-hemolytic streptococci, Staphylococcus (including MRSA), Neisseria weaveri, Eikenella corrodens, Capnocytophaga canimorsus
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.title: Systemic Infections and High-Risk Groups Following Dog Bites
items.content: Dog-bite wounds can lead to systemic infections such as bacteremia, meningitis, and sepsis, especially in immunocompromised individuals or those with compromised lymphatic drainage. Capnocytophaga canimorsus infection is particularly severe in patients with impaired hepatic function, post-splenectomy, or who are immunosuppressed.
keyPoints: Systemic infections: bacteremia, meningitis, brain abscess, endocarditis
High-risk groups: immunocompromised individuals, compromised lymphatic drainage
Capnocytophaga canimorsus infection risk in patients with impaired hepatic function, post-splenectomy, or immunosuppression
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.title: Why Cat Bites Have a High Infection Rate and Rapid Progression
items.content: Cat bites are more likely to cause infection than dog bites due to the deep penetration of their sharp teeth, leading to complications such as septic arthritis and osteomyelitis. The presence of Pasteurella multocida in the cat's oral flora contributes significantly to these infections, which tend to progress rapidly.
keyPoints: Infection occurs in more than half of all cat bite cases.
Cat bites are more likely to cause septic arthritis and osteomyelitis, especially near joints like those in the hand.
Pasteurella multocida is a common pathogen in cat-bite wound infections.
Median time from bite to infection symptoms is shorter compared to dog bites.
Infections can progress rapidly within hours, causing severe inflammation and purulent drainage.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · CAT BITES

items.title: Potential Systemic Infections and Late Consequences of Cat Bites
items.content: Cat bites can lead to systemic infections such as Pasteurella bacteremia or pneumonia, and late consequences like cat-scratch disease caused by Bartonella henselae. Other rare infections include tularemia and sporotrichosis.
keyPoints: Pasteurella may cause bacteremia or pneumonia through respiratory droplets.
Cat-bite wounds can transmit rabies or tetanus.
Bartonella henselae causes cat-scratch disease, a significant late consequence of cat bites.
Tularemia and sporotrichosis have been reported following cat bites.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · CAT BITES

items.title: Rat-Bite Fever: Key Pathogens and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Rat-bite fever is caused by Streptobacillus moniliformis or Spirillum minor, with the former being more common in the United States. It typically presents after the initial wound has healed, following an incubation period of 3–10 days for streptobacillary disease.
keyPoints: Streptobacillus moniliformis is a microaerophilic, pleomorphic gram-negative rod causing rat-bite fever in the U.S.
Spirillum minor causes similar illness mainly in Asia.
Incubation period for streptobacillary disease: 3–10 days.
Symptoms include fever, chills, myalgias, headache, severe migratory arthralgias, and a maculopapular rash involving palms and soles.
Complications can be endocarditis, myocarditis, meningitis, pneumonia, and abscesses in various organs.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · OTHER ANIMAL BITES

items.title: Diagnosis of Rat-Bite Fever: Methods and Differentials
items.content: Rat-bite fever is diagnosed by direct observation or culture of the causative organisms, with serologic testing available. Differential diagnoses include Rocky Mountain spotted fever, Lyme disease, leptospirosis, and secondary syphilis.
keyPoints: Diagnosis involves direct observation or culture on enriched media.
Serologic testing can be used for diagnosis.
Differentials: Rocky Mountain spotted fever, Lyme disease, leptospirosis, secondary syphilis.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · OTHER ANIMAL BITES

items.title: Infection Risk and Management in Human Bites
items.content: Human bites carry a significant risk of infection due to the diverse oral microflora. The depth of the wound influences infection rates, with superficial wounds having about 2% risk and deep bite wounds over 25%. Clenched-fist injuries are particularly prone to serious infections.
keyPoints: Infection risk: ~2% for superficial wounds, >25% for deep bites
Common aerobic isolates: viridans streptococci, S. aureus, E. corrodens, Haemophilus influenzae
Anaerobic species isolated in 50% of infections: Fusobacterium nucleatum, Prevotella, Porphyromonas, Peptostreptococcus
Transmission risk for various pathogens including hepatitis B/C, herpes simplex virus, syphilis, tuberculosis, actinomycosis, tetanus; low HIV transmission risk
Postexposure prophylaxis recommended for severe trauma with blood in saliva
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · HUMAN BITES

items.title: Characteristics and Complications of Clenched-Fist Injuries
items.content: Clenched-fist injuries are more common than occlusional bites and lead to serious infections due to deep tissue involvement. These injuries can inoculate bacteria into the hand's deep spaces, including bones, joints, and tendons, often resulting in delayed medical attention until infection is evident.
keyPoints: Clenched-fist injuries: more common and severe than occlusional bites
Infection risk due to deep tissue involvement (bones, joints, tendons)
E. corrodens particularly common in clenched-fist injuries
Delayed presentation often leads to serious infections; careful physical examination required
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · HUMAN BITES

items.title: When to Contact Public Health Authorities for Rabies Prophylaxis
items.content: In cases of animal bites, it's crucial to contact local and regional public-health authorities to determine if the biting species could be rabid. This step is necessary to decide whether rabies prophylaxis should be administered.
keyPoints: Contact public health authorities for potential rabies exposure
Determine if the animal species could be rabid
Consider rabies prophylaxis based on assessment
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Animal or Human Bites

items.title: Culturing Techniques and Considerations in Bite Wounds
items.content: For bite wounds, culture and Gram's staining are essential for infected wounds. Anaerobic cultures should be performed if there is evidence of abscesses, devitalized tissue, or foul-smelling exudate. For human bites, notify the microbiology lab if fastidious organisms like Eikenella corrodens are suspected.
keyPoints: Culture and Gram's staining for infected wounds
Anaerobic cultures for abscesses, devitalized tissue, or foul odor
Notify lab for fastidious organisms in human bites
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Animal or Human Bites

items.title: Approach to Bite Wound Closure
items.content: In managing bite wounds, primary closure is often avoided due to infection risks. Instead, thorough irrigation and debridement are recommended. For puncture wounds from cat bites, suturing is generally not advised because of high infection rates.
keyPoints: Primary closure is controversial for bite injuries due to infection risk.
Puncture wounds from cat bites should be left unsutured due to a high infection rate.
Facial wounds may be sutured after thorough cleaning due to good blood supply and cosmetic considerations.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · WOUND MANAGEMENT

items.title: Timing for Primary Closure of Bite Wounds
items.content: The timing of primary closure is crucial in bite wound management. Wounds older than 12 hours (arm or leg) or 24 hours (face) should not be closed primarily and may require prophylactic antibiotics.
keyPoints: Wounds >12 h old for arm/leg bites and >24 h old for facial bites should not be closed primarily.
Prophylactic antibiotics may be needed if primary closure is delayed.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · WOUND MANAGEMENT

items.title: Antibiotic Choices for Bite-Wound Infections
items.content: For established bite-wound infections, antibiotics should be selected based on the biting species and culture results. Dog and cat bites require coverage against S. aureus, Pasteurella species, C. canimorsus, streptococci, and oral anaerobes. Human bites need agents effective against S. aureus, H. influenzae, and β-lactamase-positive oral anaerobes.
keyPoints: Dog and cat bite antibiotics: Effective against S. aureus, Pasteurella species, C. canimorsus, streptococci, and oral anaerobes.
Human bite antibiotics: Active against S. aureus, H. influenzae, β-lactamase-positive oral anaerobes.
Preferred combinations: Extended-spectrum penicillin with a β-lactamase inhibitor (e.g., amoxicillin/clavulanic acid) or third-generation cephalosporins with anaerobic coverage.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.title: Duration and Monitoring of Antibiotic Therapy for Bite Wounds
items.content: Antibiotics are typically administered for 5–7 days, but not exceeding 14 days. Treatment response should be monitored closely; lack of improvement may necessitate alternative diagnostics or surgical intervention.
keyPoints: Typical antibiotic duration: 5–7 days, up to a maximum of 14 days.
Monitoring: Assess response to therapy; consider diagnostic alternatives and possible drainage if no response.
Extended therapy: Required for complications like osteomyelitis or septic arthritis.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.title: Prophylactic Antibiotic Use in Bite Wounds
items.content: The use of prophylactic antibiotics within 8 hours post-bite is debated. A meta-analysis showed a 50% reduction in infection rates with prophylaxis for dog bites, but clinical trials are limited.
keyPoints: Prophylactic antibiotic debate: Use within 8 hours post-bite is controversial.
Meta-analysis result: 50% reduction in infection rate with prophylactic antibiotics for dog bites.
Presumptive treatment recommended for human and monkey bites due to high infection rates.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.question: Which microorganism is most commonly associated with fulminant sepsis following a dog bite?
items.explanation: Capnocytophaga canimorsus is associated with fulminant sepsis following dog bites, especially in immunocompromised hosts.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.question: What percentage of dog-bite wounds typically become infected?
items.explanation: An estimated 15–20% of dog-bite wounds become infected.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.question: Which age group has the highest incidence rate of canine bites per 1000 population?
items.explanation: The highest incidence of canine bites is among boys 5–9 years old, with 6 bites per 1000 population.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.question: What systemic illness can result from dog bites or scratches?
items.explanation: Dog bites and scratches may result in systemic illnesses such as rabies.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.question: Which organism is most commonly implicated in infections resulting from cat bites?
items.explanation: Pasteurella multocida is a normal component of the feline oral flora and is implicated in the majority of cat-bite wound infections.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · CAT BITES

items.question: Which condition is more likely to develop from cat bites than dog bites due to deep tissue penetration?
items.explanation: Cat bites are more likely than dog bites to cause septic arthritis and osteomyelitis due to the deep penetration of the cat's narrow, sharp canine teeth.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · CAT BITES

items.question: What is a significant late consequence of cat bites or scratches?
items.explanation: Infection with Bartonella henselae causes cat-scratch disease and is an important late consequence of cat bites and scratches.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · CAT BITES

items.question: Which organism is most commonly associated with infections from bites of Old World monkeys?
items.explanation: Old World monkeys may transmit B virus, which can cause serious CNS infections in humans.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · OTHER ANIMAL BITES

items.question: What is the typical incubation period for streptobacillary rat-bite fever?
items.explanation: Streptobacillary disease follows an incubation period of 3–10 days.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · OTHER ANIMAL BITES

items.question: Which organism is often reported in infected wounds from bites by horses, pigs, and sheep?
items.explanation: Actinobacillus lignieresii is often reported in infected wounds from bites by horses, pigs, and sheep.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · OTHER ANIMAL BITES

items.question: Which organism is particularly common in infections from clenched-fist injuries?
items.explanation: E. corrodens is particularly common in clenched-fist injury infections.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · HUMAN BITES

items.question: What percentage of wound infections due to human bites involve anaerobic species?
items.explanation: Anaerobic species are isolated from 50% of wound infections due to human bites.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · HUMAN BITES

items.question: What is the risk of infection in superficial human-bite wounds?
items.explanation: The risk of wound infection ranges from about 2% in superficial wounds.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · HUMAN BITES

items.question: Which of the following factors should be considered when assessing a bite wound for potential rabies prophylaxis?
options: The type of biting animal and whether it could be rabid.
The color of the wound exudate.
items.explanation: Contacting local public-health authorities to determine if an individual species could be rabid is crucial in assessing the need for rabies prophylaxis.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Animal or Human Bites

items.question: What should be done when a bite wound shows signs of infection such as redness, exudate, and foul odor?
options: Ignore if the patient is not symptomatic.
items.explanation: Culture and Gram's staining of infected wounds are essential to identify causative organisms and guide appropriate treatment.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Animal or Human Bites

items.question: In which scenario is it reasonable to culture samples from an apparently uninfected wound?
options: When the bite was inflicted by a dog.
When the bite was inflicted by a cat.
When the bite was inflicted by animals other than dogs and cats.
Only if the patient requests it.
items.explanation: Culturing samples from apparently uninfected wounds is reasonable for bites by animals other than dogs and cats due to unpredictable microorganisms.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Animal or Human Bites

items.question: Which type of wound is recommended to be left unsutured due to a high infection rate?
options: Infected bite wounds on the arm
items.explanation: Puncture wounds due to cat bites should be left unsutured because of the high rate at which they become infected.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · WOUND MANAGEMENT

items.question: What is the recommended management for facial wounds that are bitten?
options: Leave them to close by secondary intention
Do not suture and leave unsutured
Suture after thorough cleaning and irrigation
items.explanation: Facial wounds are usually sutured after thorough cleaning and irrigation because of the importance of a good cosmetic result in this area.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · WOUND MANAGEMENT

items.question: When should primary closure generally be avoided for bite wounds?
options: For all facial wounds regardless of age
For wounds >12 h old on the arm or leg
For any wound that is sutured immediately
items.explanation: Wounds >12 h old (for bites to the arm or leg) should not be closed primarily and may require prophylactic antibiotics.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · WOUND MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which antibiotic combination is recommended for established infections from dog and cat bites?
items.explanation: Amoxicillin/clavulanic acid or ampicillin/sulbactam are recommended for their coverage against S. aureus, Pasteurella species, and other pathogens.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.question: Which antibiotic is NOT typically recommended as a single agent due to variable activity against certain pathogens?
items.explanation: Azithromycin alone should be avoided due to variable activity against P. multocida, E. corrodens, and fusobacteria.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.question: For the management of C. canimorsus bacteremia, which initial treatment is recommended?
options: IV penicillin G or IV ampicillin/sulbactam for 2 weeks
items.explanation: Initial treatment with IV penicillin G or ampicillin/sulbactam is recommended for C. canimorsus bacteremia.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.question: Which antibiotic regimen is suggested for seal finger?
items.explanation: Doxycycline is used for seal finger, with the duration guided by response to therapy.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.statement: Most dog-bite infections are caused by a single type of microorganism.
items.explanation: The microbiology of dog-bite wound infections is usually mixed, involving multiple types of organisms.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.statement: Children under 4 years old are more likely to sustain bites on the head or neck.
items.explanation: Among children <4 years old, two-thirds of all these injuries involve the head or neck.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.statement: Cat-bite wound infections typically have a longer median time from bite to the appearance of signs and symptoms compared to dog bites.
items.explanation: The median time from bite to the appearance of signs and symptoms of wound infection is much shorter for cat bites than for dog bites.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · CAT BITES

items.statement: Pasteurella infections can result in severe inflammation with purulent drainage.
items.explanation: Pasteurella infections tend to advance rapidly, often causing severe inflammation accompanied by purulent drainage.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · CAT BITES

items.statement: Rat-bite fever can be caused by both Streptobacillus moniliformis and Spirillum minor.
items.explanation: Streptobacillus moniliformis causes streptobacillary rat-bite fever, while Spirillum minor is responsible for sodoku.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · OTHER ANIMAL BITES

items.statement: Most cases of rat-bite fever in the United States are caused by Spirillum minor.
items.explanation: The majority of rat-bite fever cases in the U.S. are streptobacillary, not spirillar.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · OTHER ANIMAL BITES

items.statement: Human bites have a higher risk of infection than bites from other animals.
items.explanation: Human-bite wounds become infected more frequently (~10–15% of the time) than do bites inflicted by other animals.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · HUMAN BITES

items.statement: There is a high risk of HIV transmission through human bites.
items.explanation: It is biologically possible to transmit HIV through human bites, although the risk is quite low.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · HUMAN BITES

items.statement: A general physical examination should include an assessment of vital signs and evaluation for lymphangitis, lymphadenopathy, dermatologic lesions, and functional limitations.
items.explanation: The passage specifies that a general physical examination should assess these factors.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Animal or Human Bites

items.statement: Radiographs are unnecessary for all bite wounds regardless of the type of injury.
items.explanation: Radiographs should be obtained in penetrating wounds to evaluate fractures or retained foreign bodies.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Animal or Human Bites

items.statement: All abscesses associated with bite wounds should be drained.
items.explanation: The passage states that all abscesses should be drained as part of wound management.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · WOUND MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Primary closure is recommended for facial wounds older than 24 hours.
items.explanation: Facial wounds >24 h old should not be closed primarily according to the passage.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · WOUND MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Penicillin resistance in P. multocida infections is common.
items.explanation: Penicillin resistance in P. multocida is uncommon.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.statement: Antibiotics for bite wounds are typically administered for more than 14 days.
items.explanation: Antibiotics are generally given for about 5–7 days and no more than 14 days.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.assertion: Capnocytophaga canimorsus infections are particularly severe in hosts with impaired hepatic function.
items.reason: This organism may result in fulminant sepsis, especially in those with compromised liver function or immunosuppression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why infections can be severe.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.subtopic: Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES
items.assertion: Dog bites rarely result in systemic illnesses.
items.reason: Most dog-bite infections remain localized to the area of injury.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because dog bites can lead to systemic illnesses such as rabies and tetanus.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Infectious Complications of Bites | DOG BITES

items.assertion: Clenched-fist injuries often result in serious infections.
items.reason: The deep spaces of the hand are frequently inoculated with organisms during such injuries.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · HUMAN BITES

items.assertion: Injuries to the hand from bites warrant consultation with a hand surgeon.
items.reason: To assess tendon, nerve, and muscular damage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why consultation is necessary.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · Animal or Human Bites

items.assertion: Azithromycin alone is recommended as first-line therapy for bite-wound infections.
items.reason: In vitro data suggest that azithromycin provides coverage against most commonly isolated bite-wound pathogens.
items.explanation: Although azithromycin covers many pathogens, it has variable activity against certain bacteria and should be avoided unless no alternative is available.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY

items.assertion: Prophylactic antibiotics are universally recommended for all bite wounds.
items.reason: Studies have demonstrated a reduction in infection rates with prophylactic antibiotics.
items.explanation: While studies show benefits, the use of prophylactic antibiotics is controversial and depends on factors like species and wound severity.
items.reference: Infectious Complications of Bites · ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY
